content.text: What It Is

A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is a structured risk analysis process required under GDPR Article 35 when processing operations are likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. For GenAI systems, this means conducting a DPIA before deploying any AI feature or system that processes personal data in ways that meet the Article 35 trigger criteria. The trigger assessment is the pre-DPIA screening step that determines whether a full DPIA is mandatory.

The trigger assessment is particularly important for GenAI because many AI deployments sit on the boundary of existing DPIA guidance: they process personal data at scale, often involve automated analysis of individuals, use novel technology, and may combine data from multiple sources in ways that generate new inferences about individuals. The WP29/EDPB guidelines on DPIAs identify nine criteria, and the presence of two or more typically makes a DPIA mandatory.
Why It Matters

GDPR Article 35 makes DPIAs legally mandatory for high-risk processing, not optional. Deploying a GenAI system that meets the trigger criteria without a completed DPIA is a direct GDPR violation, with potential fines up to 4% of global annual turnover. National supervisory authorities in EU member states also publish lists of processing types that always require a DPIA regardless of the general criteria, and many of these lists explicitly include AI-based automated decision-making and biometric processing.

Beyond legal compliance, the trigger assessment disciplines the design process. A team that must ask whether their system requires a DPIA is forced to articulate exactly what personal data is processed, by whom, for what purpose, and with what automated logic. This articulation often surfaces privacy risks early when they are cheapest to address.
Benefits
Identifies which GenAI deployments require a DPIA before processing begins, preventing after-the-fact compliance remediation
Creates a structured forcing function for data mapping early in the product development lifecycle
Establishes a documented pre-assessment record that demonstrates the organization considered DPIA obligations seriously
Enables proportionate resource allocation: not every AI feature requires the full DPIA process, and the trigger assessment correctly scopes the obligation
Surfaces privacy-by-design opportunities: features that almost trigger a DPIA criterion benefit from design changes that keep them below the threshold
Provides the foundation for satisfying Art. 35(10) consultation obligations with supervisory authorities when DPIA risks cannot be mitigated
Trade-offs
Threshold interpretation:
The Article 35 criteria require judgment; overly strict interpretation leads to unnecessary DPIAs, while overly permissive interpretation misses genuine obligations
Pre-assessment resource cost:
A rigorous trigger assessment takes time; product teams under delivery pressure may push back unless the assessment is streamlined
DPIA scope creep:
Starting a DPIA when only a trigger assessment was needed wastes resources; having clear process separation between screening and full assessment prevents this
Jurisdictional variation:
DPIA trigger lists vary by EU member state supervisory authority; a multinational organization must track multiple lists, some of which explicitly include specific AI use cases
How It Works

The trigger assessment is a structured checklist evaluation applied to each new GenAI system or material change to an existing system. It follows a three-step logic: first confirm that personal data is processed at all; then evaluate the Article 35 criteria; then consult the applicable supervisory authority mandatory-DPIA list.
Step 1: Does the system process personal data?

Personal data under GDPR Article 4(1) is any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. For GenAI systems, common categories include: user-provided content in prompts that contains names, email addresses, or other identifiers; retrieved documents that contain personal information; model outputs that identify or describe specific individuals; logs and telemetry that link interactions to user accounts; and training data that contains personal information about real individuals.
Step 2: Evaluate the Article 35 Criteria

GDPR Article 35(3) and WP29 guidelines identify nine criteria. The presence of two or more criteria in a single processing operation typically makes a DPIA mandatory. For GenAI systems, the most commonly triggered criteria are:
Systematic evaluation:

AI systems that analyze, score, or evaluate individuals based on personal attributes — creditworthiness, health status, performance, behavior, location, or preferences — trigger this criterion. A recruitment screening AI that scores candidates based on resume content is a clear example.
Automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effect:

If the AI output is used to make or materially influence decisions that have legal or contractual consequences for individuals — loan approval, employment, insurance, benefits eligibility — this criterion is triggered. GDPR Article 22 rights apply.
Large-scale processing of special category data:

Health, biometric, genetic, racial or ethnic origin, political opinion, religious belief, sexual orientation, or criminal record data processed at scale triggers this criterion. Medical summarization AI, sentiment analysis applied to health communications, or emotion-detection AI all potentially apply.
Systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas:
AI systems that monitor behavior in public or semi-public digital environments — social media monitoring, behavioral analytics on a public website — trigger this criterion.
Novel technology:

GDPR Recital 91 identifies novel technology as a trigger factor. Generative AI is explicitly acknowledged as novel by multiple supervisory authorities. This is one of the most commonly applicable criteria for GenAI deployments.
Biometric data for identification:
AI systems using facial recognition, voice biometrics, or other biometric identifiers to verify or identify individuals trigger this criterion.
Children's data:
Any AI system likely to be used by or about individuals under 18 triggers heightened scrutiny and typically requires a DPIA.
Data combination creating new categories:
AI systems that combine datasets from multiple sources to derive new inferences about individuals — interest profiling from browsing data and purchase history — trigger this criterion.
Step 3: Supervisory Authority Mandatory Lists

Each EU member state supervisory authority publishes a list of processing types that always require a DPIA in that jurisdiction, regardless of the general criteria. These lists increasingly include AI-specific categories. The German DSK, French CNIL, UK ICO, and Dutch AP all have published DPIA lists that include automated decision-making about individuals, large-scale profiling, biometric processing, and AI-based scoring systems. Organizations operating in multiple EU jurisdictions must check each relevant national list.
Architecture

attrs.alt: DPIA Trigger Assessment Decision Flow for GenAI Systems

content.text: The diagram shows the three-step trigger assessment: confirmation of personal data processing, evaluation of Article 35 criteria (with the nine criteria listed), and the outcome branching to no DPIA required, lightweight risk assessment, or mandatory DPIA. Failure to complete a mandatory DPIA before processing begins is a direct GDPR violation.
When to Use It
Before deploying any new GenAI system that processes personal data, as part of the product development gate process
When making a material change to an existing AI system that could change its risk profile — adding a new data source, expanding the user population, adding automated decision-making
When a new supervisory authority DPIA list is published that may now cover an existing system
When a data breach or security incident reveals that an AI system processes personal data in ways that were not previously documented
During annual review of the AI system inventory, to catch systems that were deployed before the current DPIA policy was in place
How to Implement
Create a pre-assessment questionnaire

Design a short questionnaire (10-15 questions) that maps to the Article 35 criteria. Route it through the product development process so that all new AI features and systems complete the questionnaire before the design is finalized. The questionnaire output is a recommendation: no DPIA required, lightweight privacy risk assessment recommended, or full DPIA mandatory.
Integrate into the development process

The trigger assessment should happen at the design phase, not post-launch. Embed it in your product development framework as a required step before a feature enters development — typically during the design review or privacy review gate. The DPO or privacy team reviews the questionnaire output and makes the final DPIA determination.
Maintain a DPIA register

Keep a register of all AI systems that have been assessed, the outcome of each assessment, and whether a full DPIA was completed. Update the register when systems change materially. The register is an evidence artifact for regulatory inquiries and demonstrates systematic compliance with Article 35 obligations.
Examples

## DPIA Trigger Assessment — Pre-Assessment Questionnaire

**System name:** [name]
**Version / change description:** [what changed]
**Data controller / product team:** [team]
**Assessment date:** [date]
**Assessor:** [name and role]

---

### Section 1: Personal Data Processing

Q1. Does this system collect, store, access, or generate output that includes
    information relating to identified or identifiable individuals?
    [ ] Yes  [ ] No  [ ] Uncertain

Q2. What categories of personal data are processed?
    [ ] Name / contact details      [ ] Financial data
    [ ] Health / medical data       [ ] Biometric data
    [ ] Behavioral / preference data [ ] Location data
    [ ] Children's data (under 18)  [ ] Special category (Art.9)
    [ ] Other: _______________

If Q1 is No: assessment complete — no DPIA required. Stop here.

---

### Section 2: Article 35 Criteria

For each criterion below, indicate whether it applies to this system:

Q3. Does the system evaluate, score, or analyze individuals based on
    personal characteristics (behavior, preferences, performance)?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Systematic evaluation
    [ ] No

Q4. Does the system make or materially influence automated decisions
    that have legal or similarly significant effects on individuals?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Automated decisions + legal effect (Art.22)
    [ ] No

Q5. Does the system process special-category data (Art.9) at scale?
    (large-scale = more than a few hundred individuals, or the
    organization's entire user base, or a geographic region)
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Large-scale special-category data
    [ ] No

Q6. Does the system use AI or machine learning not previously deployed
    in the organization in the same way?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Novel technology (Recital 91)
    [ ] No

Q7. Does the system use biometric data to identify individuals?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Biometric identification
    [ ] No

Q8. Is the system likely to be used by or about individuals under 18?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Children's data
    [ ] No

Q9. Does the system combine data from multiple sources to derive
    new inferences about individuals?
    [ ] Yes → Criterion: Data combination / profiling
    [ ] No

---

### Section 3: Supervisory Authority Lists

Q10. Has the applicable supervisory authority published a mandatory
     DPIA list, and does this system match any item on that list?
     (Check lists for each relevant EU member state)
     [ ] Yes → DPIA mandatory regardless of criteria count
     [ ] No

---

### Outcome

Criteria triggered: [count]

Outcome:
  0 criteria → No DPIA required; standard privacy notice review recommended
  1 criterion → Lightweight privacy risk assessment recommended
  2+ criteria → Full DPIA mandatory; DPO review required before processing
  SA list match → Full DPIA mandatory; DPO review required before processing
Real-World Example

A healthcare technology company built an AI assistant that helps clinicians draft discharge summaries from unstructured clinical notes. The pre-assessment questionnaire identified: special-category health data processed at scale (criterion 5 triggered), novel AI technology (criterion 6 triggered), and a match on the German DSK mandatory DPIA list for AI-based medical data processing (criterion 10 triggered).

Three criteria triggered a mandatory DPIA. The product team initiated the full DPIA process before starting development, which led to three design changes: the model runs on a self-hosted deployment within the hospital's data environment rather than a cloud LLM API, clinical notes are not retained after the summary is generated, and the output always goes to a clinician for review before entering the medical record. These changes reduced the residual risk sufficiently for the DPO to approve the system.
Common Mistakes
Applying the trigger assessment after launch:
The DPIA must be completed before processing begins; a retroactive DPIA does not satisfy the Article 35 obligation
Treating 'no identifiers in the prompt' as equivalent to 'no personal data':
Personal data includes indirectly identifying information; an AI that processes behavioral patterns or health status of individuals handles personal data even without names
Checking only one supervisory authority list:
Multinational organizations must check the list for every relevant jurisdiction where personal data subjects are located
Not reassessing after material changes:
Adding a new data source, expanding geographic scope, or changing the model can change the trigger assessment outcome; there is no one-and-done
Conflating the trigger assessment with the DPIA itself:
The trigger assessment determines whether a DPIA is needed; the full DPIA is a separate, more detailed document
Best Practices
Make the trigger assessment a mandatory gate in the product development process, not a post-launch check
Keep the questionnaire short — 10 to 15 questions — so teams complete it honestly rather than gaming it to avoid a DPIA
Have the DPO make the final determination on borderline cases rather than leaving it to the product team
Maintain a register of all assessed systems with outcomes so you have evidence of systematic assessment for regulators
Track supervisory authority mandatory DPIA lists and update the questionnaire when new items are added
Train product managers and AI engineers on the Article 35 criteria so they can do a rough first-pass assessment before engaging the privacy team
Performance & Scalability

In organizations with many AI product teams, the trigger assessment must be lightweight enough to complete in under an hour for straightforward cases while still being rigorous enough to catch genuine DPIA obligations. The questionnaire approach scales well: the form can be self-administered by product teams, with DPO review triggered automatically when the questionnaire output indicates a likely obligation. Privacy-engineering tooling such as OneTrust or TrustArc can automate the questionnaire distribution, outcome routing, and register maintenance.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Privacy management platforms:
OneTrust, TrustArc, and Transcend provide DPIA workflow tooling with questionnaire templates, outcome routing, and register management
EDPB guidelines:

The European Data Protection Board's Guidelines 05/2022 on the use of facial recognition technology and Guidelines 02/2022 on data subjects' rights — automated decision-making provide AI-specific DPIA trigger guidance
Supervisory authority tools:
The French CNIL publishes a DPIA software tool (PIA tool) that is free, open-source, and covers the Article 35 criteria
ICO AI guidance:
The UK ICO's Explaining decisions made with AI guidance and DPIA guidance for AI are practical references even for EU GDPR compliance due to their detail

Security & Reliability

The trigger assessment itself handles sensitive design information about AI systems under development. The questionnaire responses and assessment outcomes should be stored in the privacy management system with access controls limited to the DPO, the product team, and Legal. Do not circulate pre-assessment questionnaires via unmanaged email threads where they could be forwarded outside the intended audience.

The trigger assessment process must be reliable in the sense that it catches all mandatory DPIA cases — false negatives are more costly than false positives. Calibrate the questionnaire annually by reviewing all DPIAs completed in the prior year and checking whether the trigger assessment correctly predicted their necessity.
Key Takeaways
The DPIA trigger assessment is a mandatory pre-screen for any GenAI system that processes personal data under GDPR jurisdiction

Article 35 is triggered by two or more of nine criteria: systematic evaluation, automated decisions with legal effect, large-scale special-category data, systematic monitoring, novel technology, biometric identification, children's data, data combination, and supervisory authority list matches
Novel technology (Recital 91) is one of the most commonly applicable criteria for GenAI and has been explicitly acknowledged as a trigger by multiple supervisory authorities
The DPIA must be completed before processing begins, not retroactively after deployment
Multinational organizations must check the mandatory DPIA lists of each relevant EU member state supervisory authority
Maintain a DPIA register documenting all assessed systems and outcomes to demonstrate systematic compliance
Integrate the trigger assessment into the product development gate process so it happens at the design phase, not at launch
